theme: The office is alive — now make every pixel earn its rent
visual_observations: Screenshots captured 11:01 UTC at 1280x800 desktop and 375x812 mobile. Vibe: Crushing It with FULL SYNC banner active. Key observations: (1) Luna STILL appears in two locations simultaneously — at her desk in the upper-left quadrant with moon emoji clearly visible AND as a wandering idle character in the lower floor with large bold LUNA text. This dual-render bug was flagged in review #40 and remains unfixed. (2) The wandering idle character name text is outsized — LUNA renders in large purple bold that dominates the lower floor visual hierarchy, overpowering the office floor grid. (3) The SHIP_IT floating task card (white paper-texture card, lower-left, partially overlapping the office boundary) is visible in the live deployed site but absent from local /workspace/src/Office.jsx — code drift. (4) Vibe Weather Widget is implemented in code but effectively invisible at opacity: 0.55 and tiny font-size — the sun icon near the cactus is too faint to register at a glance. (5) Mobile still has approximately 35% dead dark space below the office floor. The media query rules exist but the office container does not grow to fill the available viewport height. (6) The office floor on desktop is well-composed — six desks, Bloberto CVO at top, wall clock ticking, desk lamps glowing amber, desk monitor screens showing worker-specific SVG content, trophy and FULL SYNC banner all rendering correctly. Review #40 implementations are solid. (7) Conference table visible center-floor with no meeting badge since only one distinct idle worker is present. (8) Window sky view (daylight gradient) visible top-right. (9) Plants at right-side and bottom-left add nice environmental depth. Overall the office reads like a living space — remaining polish gaps are around the wandering character presentation and mobile scale.

ideas.title: Luna Dual-Render Bug — One Luna, One Office
ideas.description: Luna appears simultaneously at her working desk AND as a wandering idle character. This was flagged in review #40 and remains unfixed. Root cause investigation: (1) Add console.warn in Office.jsx when the same worker.id appears in both workingWorkers and idleWorkers arrays. (2) Verify workers.json / live API does not return Luna twice with different statuses. (3) Add a deduplication pass at the top of the Office component: const deduped = workers.filter((w, i, arr) => arr.findIndex(x => x.id === w.id) === i) and use deduped throughout. (4) If it is a data race condition, add a stable snapshot: const stableWorkers = useMemo(() => workers, [JSON.stringify(workers.map(w => w.id + w.status))]). The desk element renders nameplate and lamp for occ.idle workers even though the Character SVG is correctly guarded — verify there is no path where the Character is rendered at the desk position for an idle worker. WHY: Two Lunas on one office floor breaks the fundamental fiction that this is a real team map. Every visitor who opens this product sees the bug immediately. It destroys trust in the data accuracy the whole product is built on.

ideas.title: Wandering Character Name — Shrink the Giant Floor Text
ideas.description: Idle wandering characters render their firstName via .char__name with standard sizing, but without a containing desk frame the name floats freely at full scale. In the screenshot, LUNA appears in large bold purple text larger than the whiteboard text, nameplates, and the CVO title combined. Fix in Office.css: .char--idle .char__name { font-size: 0.32rem; opacity: 0.55; letter-spacing: 0.1em; transition: opacity 0.2s ease; } and .char--idle:hover .char__name { opacity: 1; }. On mobile: @media (max-width: 480px) { .char--idle .char__name { display: none; } }. The idle-duration timer should remain visible. WHY: The wandering character exists to show motion and life, not to render a banner. Shrinking the name makes wandering feel ambient and organic instead of like an escaped UI label dominating the floor.

ideas.title: Mobile Dead Space Fix — Office Floor Fills the Viewport
ideas.description: Carry-forward from reviews #39 and #40, still unfixed after two sprints. On mobile 375x812, the office floor occupies roughly 65% of viewport with 35% dead dark space below. The existing @media (max-width: 480px) block sets min-height: 280px; max-height: 320px which is too restrictive. Three-part fix: (1) In Office.css update the 480px block: .office-floor { min-height: 0; max-height: none; height: calc(100vw * 0.75); } to let the floor use a 4:3 aspect ratio based on viewport width. (2) In App.css add @media (max-width: 480px) { .tab-content { padding: 0.5rem 0.25rem; } .header { padding: 0.4rem 0.75rem; } .tab-toggle { margin: 0.3rem 0 0.5rem; } } to reclaim vertical pixels above the floor. (3) Add @media (max-width: 480px) { .office-wrap { padding: 0.25rem; } } to reduce frame padding. WHY: Mobile is likely the primary access pattern for quick team status checks. A postage-stamp office surrounded by wasted screen is embarrassing for a product whose hero is the visual office metaphor.

ideas.title: Vibe Weather Widget Presence — Make the Oracle Actually Readable
ideas.description: The vibe-weather widget is implemented in both JSX and CSS but is effectively invisible in practice. At opacity: 0.55 and font-size: 0.85rem it disappears into the floor near the cactus. Three changes in Office.css: (1) .vibe-weather { font-size: 1.1rem; opacity: 0.78; filter: drop-shadow(0 0 4px rgba(255,255,255,0.2)); } (2) For urgent vibes: .vibe-weather--on-fire, .vibe-weather--crushing { filter: drop-shadow(0 0 6px rgba(251,191,36,0.5)); } (3) In Office.jsx add title attribute: title={'Forecast: ' + vibe} to the vibe-weather div. WHY: A weather oracle that nobody can see is wasted JSX. This widget is a two-second read of team emotional state — one of the best ambient ideas in the product — but it needs to be findable before it can delight anyone.

ideas.title: Task Card Code-Drift — Spec and Style the Floor Task Floater
ideas.description: The live deployed site shows a floating paper-texture card labeled SHIP_IT / 14% in the lower-left corner of the office, partially overlapping the floor boundary. This feature does NOT exist in /workspace/src/Office.jsx — it is pure code drift. The concept is excellent: a floating task card gives the work spatial presence on the office floor. Spec to formalize: In Office.jsx add a TaskCard component that accepts taskName and progress props. CSS: position: absolute; left: 2%; bottom: 8%; z-index: 2; background: rgba(15,10,30,0.88); border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.1); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 8px; font-size: 0.38rem; backdrop-filter: blur(6px); min-width: 52px. Show taskName truncated to 14 chars, a 2px-tall mini progress bar (reuse wb-progress-fill styling), and the percentage. Add data-vibe for reactive border: .task-card[data-vibe='on-fire'] { border-color: rgba(239,68,68,0.4); } .task-card[data-vibe='crushing'] { border-color: rgba(167,139,250,0.4); }. Render in the Office return when workingWorkers.length > 0 and workingWorkers[0].task exists. WHY: This feature already lives in the deployed product but is invisible to local dev. Making it official ensures it is spec-driven, accessible, and dark-theme consistent.

ideas.title: After-Hours Starfield Floor — Four Reviews, Ten CSS Lines, No Excuses
ideas.description: Carry-forward from reviews #38, #39, #40 — four sprints, still not shipped. Zero JSX additions required. In Office.css add a ::after pseudo-element on .office-floor[data-vibe='after-hours'] with: position: absolute; inset: 0; pointer-events: none; z-index: 0; background-image composed of twelve radial-gradient star points: radial-gradient(circle 1.5px at 7% 14%, rgba(255,255,255,0.55) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 19% 38%, rgba(200,200,255,0.4) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1.5px at 34% 9%, rgba(255,255,255,0.5) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 52% 22%, rgba(180,180,255,0.45) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1.5px at 68% 47%, rgba(255,255,255,0.55) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 81% 11%, rgba(200,200,255,0.4) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1.5px at 12% 61%, rgba(255,255,255,0.5) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 43% 72%, rgba(180,180,255,0.4) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1.5px at 74% 68%, rgba(255,255,255,0.45) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 91% 33%, rgba(200,200,255,0.4) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1.5px at 28% 85%, rgba(255,255,255,0.5) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 60% 90%, rgba(180,180,255,0.4) 1px, transparent 1px). Add animation: starfield-twinkle 4s ease-in-out infinite and @keyframes starfield-twinkle { 0%, 100% { opacity: 0.6; } 50% { opacity: 1; } }. WHY: After-hours is the loneliest vibe. The floor should stop being a grid and become a universe to stare into. Four reviews. Zero JSX. Ship it.

ideas.title: Desk Nameplate Progress Glow — Momentum Visible From Across the Floor
ideas.description: Working characters have progress rings on their avatar but the desk nameplate below them is completely static. Add spatial momentum: when a worker hits 75%+ progress, their nameplate glows in their role color. In Office.jsx pass data-progress-tier to the desk element: set to 'high' when isWorking and occ.worker.progress >= 75, set to 'mid' when >= 50. In Office.css: .desk__nameplate--active[data-progress-tier='high'] { color: var(--role-color, #a78bfa); text-shadow: 0 0 6px color-mix(in srgb, var(--role-color, #a78bfa) 60%, transparent); animation: nameplate-throb 1.8s ease-in-out infinite; } .desk__nameplate--active[data-progress-tier='mid'] { opacity: 0.9; } @keyframes nameplate-throb { 0%, 100% { opacity: 0.85; } 50% { opacity: 1; } }. WHY: Progress right now only lives in the ring above the character. The desk — the worker territory — should reflect their momentum. A glowing nameplate readable from across the floor tells a story before you hover anything.

ideas.title: Ghost Desk Dust Aura — Abandoned Desks Collect Memories
ideas.description: Carry-forward from reviews #39 and #40. Ghost desks (.desk--ghost) are faded but static — they look broken rather than absent. Zero new JSX required. In Office.css add: .desk--ghost::before { content: ''; position: absolute; inset: -4px; border-radius: 8px; background: radial-gradient(ellipse 80% 60% at 50% 110%, rgba(148,163,184,0.12), transparent 70%); animation: ghost-dust 5s ease-in-out infinite; pointer-events: none; z-index: 0; } @keyframes ghost-dust { 0%, 100% { opacity: 0.4; transform: scale(1); } 50% { opacity: 0.8; transform: scale(1.04); } } and .desk--ghost::after { content: '\00B7 \00B7 \00B7'; position: absolute; top: -10px; left: 50%; transform: translateX(-50%); font-size: 0.4rem; color: rgba(148,163,184,0.35); letter-spacing: 0.3em; animation: desk-dust-float 7s ease-in-out infinite; pointer-events: none; } @keyframes desk-dust-float { 0%, 80%, 100% { opacity: 0; transform: translateX(-50%) translateY(0); } 40% { opacity: 0.7; transform: translateX(-50%) translateY(-6px); } }. WHY: OOO is the office saying someone used to sit here. Two CSS pseudo-elements turn absence into presence.

ideas.title: Idle Character Ambient Micro-Bubbles — Wanderers Have Inner Lives
ideas.description:
Carry-forward from review #40. Idle characters wander silently with tooltips only on hover. Add ambient personality: in the Character component add const [idleBubble, setIdleBubble] = useState(null). In a useEffect watching variant and wanderIdx: when variant is 'idle', fire setInterval at 30000ms. Each tick picks an emoji for the wander path (1: coffee cup, 2: thought bubble, 3: window pane, 4: lotus) and calls setIdleBubble with that emoji, then clears it after 2200ms. Render {idleBubble && variant === 'idle' &&
{idleBubble}
}. CSS: .idle-micro-bubble { position: absolute; top: -12px; left: 50%; transform: translateX(-50%); font-size: 0.5rem; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.55); border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.08); border-radius: 8px; padding: 1px 5px; white-space: nowrap; animation: micro-bubble 2.2s ease-out forwards; pointer-events: none; z-index: 10; } @keyframes micro-bubble { 0% { opacity: 0; transform: translateX(-50%) translateY(4px) scale(0.8); } 18% { opacity: 1; transform: translateX(-50%) translateY(0) scale(1); } 75% { opacity: 1; } 100% { opacity: 0; transform: translateX(-50%) translateY(-8px); } }. WHY: Motion without personality is screensaver. A thought bubble every 30 seconds transforms wandering from physics into narrative.

ideas.title: Coffee Corner Idle Count Badge — How Many People Are Lingering
ideas.description:
The coffee corner shows a chatting badge when idleWorkers.length >= 2 but does not show how many people are there. A single idle worker gets nothing. In Office.jsx inside the coffee-corner div add: {idleWorkers.length >= 1 &&
{idleWorkers.length}
}. CSS: .coffee-corner__count { position: absolute; top: -6px; right: -6px; width: 13px; height: 13px; background: var(--accent, #a855f7); border: 1px solid rgba(0,0,0,0.3); border-radius: 50%; font-size: 0.38rem; font-weight: 700; color: white; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; z-index: 5; } For 3+ workers: .coffee-corner__count[data-many] { animation: count-pulse 2s ease-in-out infinite; } @keyframes count-pulse { 0%, 100% { transform: scale(1); } 50% { transform: scale(1.12); } } where data-many is set when idleWorkers.length >= 3. WHY: Right now you must count wandering characters to know the social state. A single digit badge makes idle energy readable at a glance — exactly like a chat notification but for physical presence.

ideas.title: Team Sprint Victory Lap — When Everyone Ships, the Floor Erupts in Gold
ideas.description:
Carry-forward from review #40. Individual completion toasts fire in App.jsx but there is zero office-level event when ALL workers complete simultaneously. In Office.jsx add: const allComplete = nonMgr.length > 0 && workingWorkers.length > 0 && workingWorkers.every(w => w.progress >= 100). Track rising edge with a prevAllCompleteRef and setVictoryLap(true) for 6000ms when it flips. Pass data-victory={victoryLap || undefined} to the office-floor div and render
ALL SHIPPED
inside when victoryLap is true. CSS for the floor: .office-floor[data-victory]::before { content: ''; position: absolute; inset: 0; pointer-events: none; z-index: 0; background: radial-gradient(ellipse 80% 50% at 50% 100%, rgba(251,191,36,0.18), transparent 70%); animation: victory-gold 6s ease-in-out forwards; } @keyframes victory-gold { 0% { opacity: 0; } 15% { opacity: 1; } 75% { opacity: 1; } 100% { opacity: 0; } }. CSS for the banner: .victory-banner { position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 50%; transform: translate(-50%,-50%); font-size: 0.65rem; font-weight: 900; letter-spacing: 0.18em; text-transform: uppercase; color: #fbbf24; text-shadow: 0 0 20px rgba(251,191,36,0.8); z-index: 10; pointer-events: none; animation: victory-text 6s ease forwards; white-space: nowrap; } @keyframes victory-text { 0% { opacity: 0; transform: translate(-50%,-50%) scale(0.7); } 15% { opacity: 1; transform: translate(-50%,-50%) scale(1.08); } 75% { opacity: 1; } 100% { opacity: 0; } }. During victoryLap add data-victory to Bloberto Character and: .char[data-victory] .char__avatar { animation: bloberto-victory-spin 1.2s ease-in-out 2; } @keyframes bloberto-victory-spin { 50% { transform: rotate(180deg) scale(1.2); } 100% { transform: rotate(360deg); } }. WHY: Team completion is the rarest and most earned office event. It deserves a moment people screenshot and share — the floor erupting in gold while the boss spins.

ideas.title: Whiteboard Task History Trail — The Board Has Memory
ideas.description:
The whiteboard shows current vibe text and a progress bar but has zero memory of what came before. In App.jsx maintain a completedTasks ref of the last 2 tasks that reached 100% — push worker.task in the same useEffect that fires the completion toast, capped at 2 items with shift(). Pass completedTasks prop to Office. In Office.jsx inside the whiteboard div below wb-progress-track add: {completedTasks && completedTasks.slice(0,2).map((t, i) =>
checkmark {t.slice(0,14)}
)}. CSS: .wb-history-item { font-size: 0.28rem; color: var(--text-muted); margin-top: 1px; letter-spacing: 0.05em; white-space: nowrap; overflow: hidden; text-overflow: ellipsis; line-height: 1.3; }. Hide on mobile: @media (max-width: 480px) { .wb-history-item { display: none; } }. WHY: A whiteboard in a real office accumulates. Half-erased tasks from yesterday, crossed-out items, ghost lines of completed sprints. A whiteboard with memory feels like a living workspace. A whiteboard that resets to a vibe word every time feels like a screensaver label. Ten CSS lines and a prop pipe separate these two experiences.
